text:
- Lord Denver, please, calm yourself – said Mr. Powlett evenly. – The loss of the Daughter of the Wind affects the whole Company, and I am sure Lord Ebbing was shaken when he heard the news. But since none of us can raise a ship from the sea floor, let’s focus on what comes next – he pushed his spectacles up and walked to the board for a better look. – For my part, I would say we should only let ships out every third new moon. That would be the safe choice. However… - he raised a hand as he straightened, knowing how the others would bridle at the suggestion. - …I know how much revenue the Company and our workers would lose, especially now that even that wouldn’t guarantee success. I hope you understand as well, Lord Ebbing, that families’ livelihoods are at stake here, and we can’t afford to launch ships only every three months, leaving crews to sit in the Colonies for three months before they can sail back. And that’s not even considering winter. It’s not viable business, and Parliament would hardly cheer.

Caleb and Lord Ebbing both knew this would come down to money. They were speaking to merchants, after all, though merchants who had financed a great deal of research they hoped would pay off.

- Of course I understand; nevertheless, until a new pattern establishes itself, I can’t say anything more definite.

If a pattern emerges at all. Caleb knew Lord Ebbing would have liked to add that, but it would only have soured the room. They had to look constructive.

- Then we strengthen our ships, hire a few more engineers, and if we’re fighting sea monsters, perhaps we should send a squad of soldiers on every vessel – said Mr. Powlett.

Several people laughed at that, but neither Caleb nor Lord Ebbing felt like laughing. Caleb could only imagine sea monsters as a plausible cause. He was faintly disappointed no one had even asked about causes—why there had been a pattern before, why there wasn’t one now, and what might lie behind it all. This was one reason he had never wanted to be a businessman, even though it would have been more lucrative to work for the shipping companies than for the Academy.

- I don’t think Mr. Powlett’s proposal is a bad idea – Caleb said at last.

Lord Ebbing looked at him, surprised. It was rare for his assistant to step into a high-stakes debate, but he didn’t stop him.

– Since we don’t know what we’re facing, ship safety ought to be the first priority, shouldn’t it?

- Do you know, Mr. André, what that would cost us? A hundred soldiers! And engineers work even slower than you do – said Lord Denver, whose last remark again drew general amusement from those present.

- I don’t know what it would cost, but with respect—would it be more than the value of the ships we’ve lost and the cargo aboard them? He didn’t even bring up the sailors’ lives.

- We have insurance for that. Lord Ebbing, you should enroll your assistant in an accounting course.

Caleb flushed. He would have loved to tell Denver to go to hell—that there was no point to this circus if they only took them seriously when they said what they wanted to hear. He was starting to think the whole game was to find some pretext to drag Lord Ebbing before a committee. After that, he had the sense to keep his mouth shut.

There were a few more formalities, their clear, firm positions were taken down, and half an hour later they were in Lord Ebbing’s curtained carriage, rattling over cobbles back toward the Academy.

- Remind me, Mr. André, why I still tolerate your scatterbrained habits, and why your appearance brings shame not only on our Institute and on me, but on the entire Academy as well?

Caleb had expected it. Lord Ebbing would not let the lateness pass, or the fact he was wearing yesterday’s clothes, or that he’d shown up bareheaded—even if a hat sat poorly on his spiky-cropped hair, which was sometimes shorter than his stubble.

- Because you were my father’s childhood friend, and because you once cleaned him out at cards so badly that—if he didn’t want to sell the country estate—you had to take me under your wing, despite my mother being a colonial woman – he repeated the oft-told story. – And because, despite every lapse in form, I’m an excellent assistant.

Lord Ebbing laughed.

- I suspect you haven’t lunched yet, my boy – he said, then tapped the carriage roof with his walking stick. – Drop us at the coffeehouse!

A few minutes later they stopped before an imposing building. Unlike the smaller coffeehouses, Lord Ebbing’s haunt had four stories, and most of its street-facing wall was taken up by immense, metal-framed windows. It was a favored meeting place for wealthy merchants, and any number of deals had been struck there that shaped the life of the island—and perhaps the entire Union. No one sat at the little outdoor tables; it was too cold. But when they stepped inside, the sound of lively conversation met Caleb along with the smells of smoke, food, coffee, and alcohol. A waiter greeted Lord Ebbing by name and led them up to the gallery, to a table by a window. Four green velvet armchairs had been pulled up to the round table; they each sank into one. Lord Ebbing ordered them grilled cheese sandwiches with coffee, and, hmming to himself, considered asking for a steak if they stayed long.

Caleb didn’t dare speak until Lord Ebbing had lit his pipe and was puffing peacefully, gazing out at the street. He seemed lost in thought. Caleb couldn’t keep his own thoughts in any longer.

- Sir, if I may… I dug through records all night. As I studied the dates, I ruled out every weather phenomenon I could, so I began to think perhaps some kind of sea creature prefers to attack our ships. I found plenty of accounts of leviathans, krakens, even whales turning aggressive…

Lord Ebbing smiled and hummed.

- Sir?

- Go on, Mr. André. I’m curious what you’ve come to, and how it lines up with where I ended.

- So—I still consider those possible. Perhaps this sea monster hunts on a pattern like that. But from what I could see, things changed not long after—two, at most three, cycles after—you advised the shipping companies when to send their ships. Everything worked splendidly, and then the pattern shifted, as if…

- As if whatever hunts our ships adapted – finished Lord Ebbing.

- Yes, sir. Is that possible?

- Certainly. Predator and prey coevolve all the time. It just doesn’t usually happen this fast, since the southern big cats didn’t suddenly invent gunpowder.

They set down the grilled cheese. Caleb tried not to pounce on it like the hungry predator just mentioned, and he was grateful Lord Ebbing didn’t once remark on his loudly growling stomach.

- What do you infer from all this, Mr. André? I know you’ve already seen it; you’re just reluctant to say it – the older man said, thoughtful.

Caleb kept quiet a moment, chewing slowly. Hungry as he was, the sandwich tasted better than a multi-course feast on winter solstice, and the coffee ran down his throat like a life-giving elixir. He did have a theory, but it felt too wild to voice—until now.

- That whatever is attacking our ships is intelligent. And that it hunts human ships specifically. Nothing else will do—even though, if it can sink our vessels, it could hunt leviathans as well.
